返回本批次

2026 年国誉设计奖获奖者

Winners of the 2026 Kokuyo Design Awards

spoon-tamago.com 作者 zdw 分数 47 评论 13 2026/04/06 11:32
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 国誉设计奖（以前）可以说是日本最负盛名的文具设计奖，至今已经举办了近四分之一个世纪。该奖项由拥有 120 年历史的文具公司 KOKUYO 主办，每年收到近 1500 个尚未商业化的新产品参赛作品，获奖概念有机会成为现实生活中的产品。今年的主题“哈蒙：引起共鸣的设计”要求设计师根据自己独特的生活经验提交概念，从而有可能与社会产生共鸣。上个月宣布了一名获奖者和三名优异奖。大奖：Hiroki Kannari 的“Before Note” 最高奖颁给了Before Note，它是对笔记本的看似简单却又彻底的重新思考。 Kannari 提出的不是成品，而是“前笔记本”——用户可以通过选择纸张数量和封面设计来自定义的一捆页面。这是一种介于大规模生产和个性化之间的设计，反映出个性比以往任何时候都更加重要的世界。您不是购买笔记本，而是完成它——将一个被动的物体变成一个主动的、个人的过程。
  2. 优异奖 Gram 由 Takashi Higashide 这个钢笔系列探索了几乎看不见的东西：重量。通过仅调整几克，而不改变形状或材料，它揭示了我们的书写体验可以如何微妙地改变。 gram的辉煌之处在于它的敏感性。它让用户意识到他们通常忽视的感觉，将书写转变为一种更有意识、更有触觉的行为。
  3. 由 Yuji Tsukamoto 设计的边缘识别笔记本电脑 乍一看，这些笔记本电脑看起来很简约——纯白色的封面带有安静的优雅。但关键细节在于彩色边缘，让用户一眼就能区分笔记本电脑，同时保持视觉和谐。这是组织和美学的完美平衡。更好的是，通过仅对边缘而不是整个覆盖进行着色，该设计巧妙地减少了墨水的使用，这是对可持续性的低调认可。
  4. 五十岚水树 (Mizuki Igarashi) 和泷泽拉拉 (Rara Takizawa) 的《梯度日记》 传统的规划者采用严格的盒子和整齐分开的日子。 《梯度日记》完全拒绝这种结构。使用柔和的渐变而不是线条，它允许用户根据自己的日程安排扩大或缩小书写空间。任务可以自然地在几天内流动，反映了时间的实际感觉——流动、不均匀和连续。
  5. 决赛入围者 Tasuku Denno 设计的红白包装纸 一种蜂窝结构的包装材料，可转变为装饰，延长礼品包装的使用寿命。

Original Summary

  1. The Kokuyo Design Awards, (previously) arguably Japan’s most-prestigious stationery design award, has been held for almost a quarter of a century now. Hosted by 120-year old stationery firm KOKUYO, the award receives close to 1500 entries each year for new products that have yet to be commercialized, with winning concepts given the opportunity to become real-life products. For this year’s theme—hamon: design that resonates—designers were asked to submit concepts based on their own unique, lived experience, which in turn has the potential to resonate with society. One winner and three merit awards were announced last month. Grand Prix: “Before Note” by Hiroki Kannari The top prize went to Before Note, a deceptively simple yet radical rethink of the notebook. Instead of a finished product, Kannari proposes a “pre-notebook”—a bundle of pages that users can customize themselves by choosing the number of sheets and cover design. It’s a design that sits between mass production and personalization, reflecting a world where individuality matters more than ever. Rather than buying a notebook, you complete it—turning a passive object into an active, personal process.
  2. Merit Awards Gram by Takashi Higashide This pen series explores something almost invisible: weight. By adjusting only a few grams—without changing shape or material—it reveals how subtly our writing experience can shift. The brilliance of gram lies in its sensitivity. It makes users aware of sensations they normally overlook, transforming writing into a more conscious, tactile act.
  3. Notebooks Identified by Edges by Yuji Tsukamoto At first glance, these notebooks look minimal—plain white covers with a quiet elegance. But the key detail lies in the colored edges, allowing users to distinguish notebooks at a glance while maintaining visual harmony. It’s a perfect balance of organization and aesthetics. Even better, by coloring only the edges rather than full covers, the design subtly reduces ink usage—an understated nod to sustainability.
  4. Gradience Diary by Mizuki Igarashi & Rara Takizawa Traditional planners impose rigid boxes and neatly separated days. Gradience Diary rejects that structure entirely. Using a soft gradient instead of lines, it allows users to expand or shrink their writing space depending on their schedule. Tasks can flow across days naturally, mirroring how time actually feels—fluid, uneven, and continuous.
  5. Finalists Red and White Packing Paper by Tasuku Denno A honeycomb-structured wrapping material that transforms into decoration, extending the life of gift packaging.

评论（默认中文）

supliminal 2026/04/06 13:30
> 一支笔可以产生微弱、模糊的线条，鼓励模糊性和反思，而不是大胆的确定性。

我不喜欢这么消极。鉴于这些都是享有盛誉的奖项，我期待更高的标准。除了照片中的死亡之握让我为这支可怜的笔而畏缩之外，不幸的是，这篇文章显示了整件事的自命不凡。艺术家（绘画类）自从作为粗略的一部分而存在以来，就一直在使用宽毡记号笔，特别是因为它留下了足够的模糊性，想象力可以填充细节，使他们能够在不致力于任何具体想法的情况下推进概念（参见 Syd Mead、Scott Robertson 等）。这是工作的实际部分，而不是工薪族用来进行深刻反思的文具。

> A pen that produces faint, smudged lines, encouraging ambiguity and reflection rather than bold certainty.

I dislike being this negative. Given these are prestigious awards I was expecting higher standards. Apart from the death-grip in the photo which makes me wince for the poor pen, this entry unfortunately shows the pretentiousness of the whole thing. Artists (drawing and painting kind) have been using broad felt markers for as long as they’ve been around as part of a rough-in specifically because it leaves enough ambiguity where the imagination can fill in the details, allowing them to move forward through a concept without committing to any specific idea (see Syd Mead, Scott Robertson, and so on). This is an actual part of the work, not a stationary that a salaryman uses for some profound moment of reflection.

在 HN 中查看
tedeh 2026/04/06 14:19
这篇文章充满了这样刺耳的句子：

“你不是购买笔记本，而是完成它——将一个被动的物体变成一个主动的、个人的过程。”

The article is filled with jarring sentences like this:

"Rather than buying a notebook, you complete it—turning a passive object into an active, personal process."

在 HN 中查看
gotbeans 2026/04/06 15:56
领英奖项
Linkedin awards
在 HN 中查看
thih9 2026/04/06 16:16
也许。这是主要奖项的最后一段：

> 这是一种介于大规模生产和个性化之间的设计，反映出个性比以往任何时候都更加重要的世界。您不是购买笔记本，而是完成它——将一个被动的物体变成一个主动的、个人的过程。

这是通过 translate.kagi.com 将“LinkedIn say”翻译成英文：

> 这是一种以“个性”为幌子向人们收取组装办公用品特权的方式。您购买的不仅仅是一台笔记本电脑；你正在为完成它的苦差事付出代价，这样你就可以感觉到你已经有了一个“个人过程”，而不是仅仅购买了一个该死的笔记本。

看起来很准确。

话又说回来，我还拥有一个漂亮的笔记本和一支钢笔，我喜欢使用它们，即使这只是我觉得我有一个个人过程。

Perhaps. This is the last paragraph from the main award:

> It’s a design that sits between mass production and personalization, reflecting a world where individuality matters more than ever. Rather than buying a notebook, you complete it—turning a passive object into an active, personal process.

This is a translation via translate.kagi.com from "LinkedIn speak" to English:

> It’s a way to charge people for the privilege of assembling their own office supplies under the guise of "individuality." You aren't just buying a notebook; you're paying for the chore of finishing it so you can feel like you've had a "personal process" instead of just buying a damn notebook.

Seems accurate.

Then again, I also own a nice notebook and a fountain pen and I enjoy using them, even if this is just me feeling like I have a personal process.

Is the gram pen a design for Caran D’Ache? Because it looks a lot like their 849 model

NooneAtAll3 2026/04/06 14:47
不同重量的钢笔听起来像是一个不错的艺术装置

彩色边缘笔记本可能是我会购买的产品

具有可变透明度的书签看起来像是您购买/赠送的一件很酷的东西，但最终意识到完全不切实际（但它可能值得一点）

折纸包装看起来很酷，但没有人会想到在日本/东亚以外使用，因为这是一种文化

其余的我不明白也不喜欢（还有……最后一支只是一支笔，根本没有任何噱头？嗯？）

pens with different weights sounds like a nice art installation

colored edges notebooks is probably a product I'd buy

bookmark with variable transparency looks like a cool thing you buy/gift that's eventually realized to be totally impractical (but it was probably worth it for a bit)

and origami packing looks cool, but not something anyone would even think of using outside Japan/east Asia because it's a culture thing

the rest I don't understand and don't like (also... last one is simply a pen with no gimmick at all? eh?)
